# Citations

<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6) -->

All sources referenced by the French Horn Effort Rubric and supporting reference files.
Numbered reference style. Access dates: March 2026 unless otherwise noted.

---

## Weight Data

**[1]** Matosinhos, Ricardo. "How Much Does a Horn Weigh?" *ricardomatosinhos.com*, n.d.
<https://www.ricardomatosinhos.com/index.php/en/blog/167-how-much-does-a-horn-weigh>
Crowd-sourced and maker-provided horn weight survey with model-by-model data for singles, doubles, triples, Vienna horns, and children's horns. Most granular publicly available horn weight dataset.

**[2]** "How Much Does a Clarinet Weigh in Pounds?" *Musical Instrument Hub*, n.d.
<https://www.musicalinstrumenthub.com/archives/31492>
Clarinet weight data by model (Buffet R13, Yamaha YCL-857-II, Selmer Privilege). Note: article shows signs of AI-generated content; weight figures are plausible but not confirmed against manufacturer datasheets.

**[3]** "How Much Do Brass Instruments Weigh? (16 Models Checked)." *BrassHero*, n.d.
<https://brasshero.com/how-much-brass-instruments-weigh/>
Comparative brass instrument weight table. French horn listed as 6-8 lbs, which is higher than the Matosinhos survey data (4.6-5.9 lbs for doubles). Discrepancy may reflect case inclusion or different measurement methodology. No specific horn model names given.

**[4]** "Holton H279 Farkas Professional French Horn." *holtonfrenchhorn.com*, n.d.
<https://www.holtonfrenchhorn.com/holton-h279-farkas-professional-french-horn/>
Product specifications for the H279: F/Bb, .468" bore, 12.25" detachable bell, nickel silver body, Kruspe wrap. No weight published on this page.

## Air Pressure & Respiratory Data

**[5]** Pamies-Vila, Montserrat; Hofmann, Alex; Chatziioannou, Vasileios. "Analysis of Tonguing and Blowing Actions During Clarinet Performance." *Frontiers in Psychology*, vol. 9, article 617, 2018. DOI: 10.3389/fpsyg.2018.00617.
<https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5936989/>
N=11 clarinettists. Mouth pressure: 2.7 kPa (piano) to 4.2 kPa (forte, fast tempo) in chalumeau register. Mouthpiece pressure envelope: 1.7 kPa (piano), 2.6 kPa (forte). German Bb clarinet, synthetic reed, 50 kHz sampling.

**[6]** Bouros, Evangelos; Protogerou, Vassilios; Castana, Ourania; Vasilopoulos, Georgios. "Respiratory Function in Wind Instrument Players." *Materia Socio-Medica*, vol. 30, no. 3, pp. 204-208, 2018. DOI: 10.5455/msm.2018.30.204-208.
<https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6195392/>
N=32 professional wind instrumentalists (16 brass, 16 woodwind). Pre/post-rehearsal spirometry. Wooden instrument players showed lower FVC (by 0.49 L) and FEV1 (by 0.7 L) than brass players. Tuba pressure of 60-70 mmHg for high notes cited from Elghozi et al. 2008 (not original data in this study). No original blowing pressure measurements taken.

**[7]** Wolfe, Joe. "Air Speed and Blowing Pressure in Woodwind and Brass Instruments: How Important Are They?" *School of Physics, UNSW Sydney*, n.d.
<https://www.phys.unsw.edu.au/jw/air-speed.html>
General wind instrument ranges: blowing pressure 1-10 kPa, airflow 0.1-1 L/s. Notes that clarinet is unusual — higher pitch does not require higher pressure at the same loudness. No instrument-specific pressure tables or French horn data.

**[8]** Spahn, Claudia; Hipp, Anna Maria; Schubert, Bernd; Axt, Marcus Rudolf; Stratmann, Markus; Schmolder, Christian; Richter, Bernhard. "Airflow and Air Velocity Measurements While Playing Wind Instruments, with Respect to Risk Assessment of a SARS-CoV-2 Infection." *International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health*, vol. 18, no. 10, article 5413, 2021. DOI: 10.3390/ijerph18105413.
<https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8159134/>
N=14 professional wind instrumentalists (Bamberg Symphony). Horn and clarinet: air velocity <0.1 m/s at 1 m distance. No airflows measurable beyond 1.5 m from any wind instrument. This study measured air velocity at distance, not blowing pressure.

## Ergonomics & Biomechanics

**[9]** ErgoBrass. "French Horn." *ergobrass.com*, n.d.
<https://www.ergobrass.com/en/frenchhorn/>
Testimonials documenting neck, shoulder, arm, and back pain in horn players. Triple horn weight of 5.5 kg cited in user testimonial. No quantified epidemiological data — testimonials only.

**[10]** Ericson, John. Various articles on posture, weight, and endurance. *Horn Matters*, 2008-2024.
<https://www.hornmatters.com/>
Qualitative advice on horn posture and weight. Notes that adult players develop postural imbalances including shoulder misalignment. "Hornmasters on Endurance" series cites Farkas, Yancich, and Berv protocols. No quantified biomechanical measurements.

**[11]** jennyclarinet.com — Clarinet ergonomics and thumb rest position.
*Status: Source inaccessible during research (March 2026). Claims attributed to this source in the plan could not be verified.* The "70% of new students report hand/wrist discomfort" statistic is UNVERIFIED.

**[12]** Lopez-Pineda, Javier; Rodriguez-Martinez, Maria Carmen; Gomez-Rodriguez, Rosa; Garcia-Casares, Lucia; Garcia-Casares, Natalia. "Biomechanical Assessments in Woodwind Musicians: A Systematic Review." *Healthcare (Basel)*, vol. 11, no. 11, article 1621, 2023.
<https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10253106/>
Systematic review: 16 studies, 390 participants. Clarinet finger force: 1.17 N (Weber excerpts), 0.64 N (technical exercises). Embouchure force comparison (from Clemente et al. 2019, included in review): French horn lower lip 86 g vs. clarinet 54.1 g. Brass players apply greater oral force than woodwind. MSD 12-month prevalence: 41-93%.

**[13]** Baadjou, V.A.E.; van Eijsden-Besseling, M.D.F.; Verbunt, J.A.M.C.F.; de Bie, R.A.; Geers, R.P.J.; Smeets, R.J.E.M.; Seelen, H.A.M. "Playing the Clarinet: Influence of Body Posture on Muscle Activity and Sound Quality." *Medical Problems of Performing Artists*, vol. 32, no. 3, pp. 125-131, September 2017.
<https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28988262/>
N=20 clarinet players. EMG study: experimental posture reduced upper trapezius and brachioradialis activity while increasing erector spinae and lower trapezius. Posture significantly affected thoracic and pelvic angles (p<0.001).

## Endurance & Energy

**[14]** "Building Musical Stamina." *The Real School of Music Blog*, n.d.
<https://blog.therealschoolofmusic.com/blog-home/building-musical-stamina>
Qualitative stamina-building advice. No quantitative benchmarks, durations, or percentages. Recommends scales for stamina building, keeping effort in "low to medium range."

**[15]** "Endurance." *HSU Trumpets*, n.d.
<http://www.hsutrumpets.com/endurance/>
Brass endurance principles: ~5-minute rest breaks when fatigue begins; 10-minute playing sessions as building blocks; "one hour of practice gives endurance for a two-hour rehearsal"; embouchure muscles plateau after 3 consecutive days of 1-hour practice. Note: the exact phrase "5-on/5-off protocol" is not used on this page, though the concepts align.

**[16]** Baadjou, V.A.E.; van Eijsden-Besseling, M.D.F.; Samama-Polak, A.L.W.; Smeets, R.J.E.M.; Lima Passos, V.; Westerterp, K.R. "Energy Expenditure in Brass and Woodwind Instrumentalists: The Effect of Body Posture." *Medical Problems of Performing Artists*, vol. 26, no. 4, pp. 218-223, December 2011.
<https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22211199/>
N=18 musicians (6 brass, 12 woodwind). MET: 1.69 (non-optimized posture) to 1.80 (optimized posture). Energy expenditure 23-32% above resting metabolic rate. Fatigue perception does not correlate with actual metabolic cost differences between postures.

## Cross-Instrument Correlation & Musculoskeletal

**[17]** "Things to Focus on When Switching Musical Instruments." *Melodica.ae*, n.d.
<https://melodica.ae/things-focus-switching-musical-instrument/>
General music academy blog. Notes that brass requires strong breath control and embouchure, woodwind requires finger coordination. No quantitative data. Low reliability for rubric purposes.

**[18]** Gotouda, A.; Yamaguchi, T.; Okada, K.; Matsuki, T.; Gotouda, S.; Inoue, N. "Influence of Playing Wind Instruments on Activity of Masticatory Muscles." *Journal of Oral Rehabilitation*, vol. 34, no. 9, pp. 645-651, 2007.
<https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17716263/>
N=19 brass + 14 woodwind players. EMG of masseter and temporal muscles. Brass players showed significantly higher RMS in high tone vs. tuning tone; woodwind players did not. 90-minute sustained playing produced no obvious jaw muscle fatigue (no MPF decrease). Masticatory load during playing is "very small" compared to maximum clenching.

**[19]** Visentin, Peter; Shan, Gongbing. "Applications of EMG Pertaining to Music Performance — A Review." *Arts BioMechanics*, vol. 1, no. 1, pp. 15-32, 2011.
*Status: ResearchGate page returned 403 Forbidden. Author attribution inferred from cross-referencing; not directly confirmed from source page.* Reviews EMG studies in music from 1973-2010 across skill analysis, biofeedback, and injury prevention.

**[20]** Cebria i Iranzo, M. Angels; Perez-Soriano, Pedro; Igual Camacho, Celedonia; Llana Belloch, Salvador; Cortell-Tormo, Juan M. "Playing-Related Musculoskeletal Disorders in Woodwind, Brass and Percussion Players: A Review." *Journal of Human Sport and Exercise*, vol. 5, no. 1, pp. 94-100, 2010.
*Status: ResearchGate page returned 403 Forbidden. Citation confirmed via search results.* Reports brass and oboe/bassoon at low injury rates (0-5.9%); saxophone, clarinet, flute at medium rates (6.0-11.9%). Note: the "86% prevalence" figure attributed to this source in the plan was NOT confirmed — the paper's own figures are substantially lower. The 86% figure likely originates from career-lifetime prevalence studies of all musicians, not this specific review.

## Returning/Aging Musicians

**[21]** "Can You Learn — or Relearn — an Instrument During Retirement?" *LifeStream at Glendale Blog*, n.d.
<https://www.lifestreamatglendale.com/blog/can-you-learn-or-relearn-an-instrument-during-retirement>
Senior living community blog. General encouragement about music in retirement. No specific data on lung capacity, physical challenges, or quantitative measures. Not a clinical or pedagogical resource.

**[22]** Hanna-Pladdy, Brenda; MacKay, Alicia. "The Relation Between Instrumental Musical Activity and Cognitive Aging." *Neuropsychology*, vol. 25, no. 3, pp. 378-386, May 2011. DOI: 10.1037/a0021895.
<https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4354683/>
N=70 healthy older adults (ages 60-83). Musicians with 10+ years of activity showed better nonverbal memory, naming, and executive function. 77.3% classification accuracy. Correlational design — does not establish causation.

## Additional Sources Found During Research

**[23]** Price, Kevin; Watson, Alan H.D. "Effect of Using Ergobrass Ergonomic Supports on Postural Muscles in Trumpet, Trombone, and French Horn Players." *Medical Problems of Performing Artists*, vol. 33, no. 3, pp. 183-190, September 2018.
<https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30204824/>
N=20 conservatoire students. French horn: EMG reductions of 15-30% in biceps, pectoralis major, and deltoid with ergonomic support. Demonstrates which muscles bear horn-holding load.

**[24]** van Selms, Maurits K.A.; Wiegers, Jetske W.; van der Meer, Hedwig A.; Ahlberg, Jari; Lobbezoo, Frank; Visscher, Corine M. "Temporomandibular Disorders, Pain in the Neck and Shoulder Area, and Headache Among Musicians." *Journal of Oral Rehabilitation*, vol. 47, no. 2, pp. 132-142, 2020.
<https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31520546/>
N=1,470 musicians from 50 Dutch ensembles. Neck/shoulder pain: 52.5%. TMD pain: 18.3%. Woodwind players associated with TMD pain.

**[25]** Ericson, John. "Hornmasters on Endurance, Part I." *Horn Matters*, July 2012.
<https://www.hornmatters.com/2012/07/hornmasters-on-endurance-part-i/>
Cites Farkas (two types of endurance: intermittent vs. continuous), Yancich (play without stopping, repeat etudes), Berv (all 8 Belloli Etudes in 50 minutes, Strauss First Concerto 4x). Berv: "No easy routes to endurance."

**[26]** Ranelli, Sonia; Straker, Leon; Smith, Anne. "Playing-Related Musculoskeletal Problems in Children Learning Instrumental Music." *Medical Problems of Performing Artists*, vol. 26, no. 3, pp. 123-139, September 2011.
<https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21987067/>
N=731 children (ages 7-17). Lifetime PRMP symptoms: 67%. Past-month symptoms: 56%. Right hand/elbow: 24%. Instrument type significantly associated with all PRMP (p<0.005).

---

*Total sources: 26. Verified via web fetch: 18. Partially verified: 4 ([9], [10], [15], [19]). Inaccessible: 3 ([11], [20], [21] — content not matching expectations or site blocked). Low-value: 1 ([17]).*
